title: Carbon Monoxide Poisoning: A Systematic Approach
objectives: Understand the pathophysiology of Carbon Monoxide Poisoning
Identify key diagnostic features of Carbon Monoxide Poisoning
Apply clinical reasoning to Carbon Monoxide Poisoning management
Recognize common pitfalls in Carbon Monoxide Poisoning

sections.title: Overview of Carbon Monoxide Poisoning
sections.content:
**Carbon Monoxide Poisoning**

Cherry-red appearance myth, carboxyhemoglobin levels, pulse ox falsely normal, hyperbaric oxygen

**Key Points:**
• CO binds hemoglobin with 250x affinity of O2 → carboxyhemoglobin (COHb) → tissue hypoxia
• Pulse oximetry reads FALSELY NORMAL (cannot distinguish COHb from OxHb)
• Symptoms by level: headache (10-20%), confusion (20-40%), seizures/coma (>40%), death (>60%)

sections.title: Pathophysiology & Key Features
sections.content:
• CO binds hemoglobin with 250x affinity of O2 → carboxyhemoglobin (COHb) → tissue hypoxia
• Pulse oximetry reads FALSELY NORMAL (cannot distinguish COHb from OxHb)

sections.title: Diagnosis & Management
sections.content:
• Symptoms by level: headache (10-20%), confusion (20-40%), seizures/coma (>40%), death (>60%)
• Diagnosis: co-oximetry (venous or arterial) — measures COHb directly
• Treatment: 100% O2 via NRB mask (reduces CO half-life from 5h to 1h); Hyperbaric O2 for: syncope, seizures, COHb >25%, pregnancy, cardiac ischemia

sections.title: Clinical Pearls
sections.content:
**High-Yield Points for Carbon Monoxide Poisoning:**

• Symptoms by level: headache (10-20%), confusion (20-40%), seizures/coma (>40%), death (>60%)
• Diagnosis: co-oximetry (venous or arterial) — measures COHb directly
• Treatment: 100% O2 via NRB mask (reduces CO half-life from 5h to 1h); Hyperbaric O2 for: syncope, seizures, COHb >25%, pregnancy, cardiac ischemia

mcqs.question: Which of the following is MOST characteristic of Carbon Monoxide Poisoning?

options.text: CO binds hemoglobin with 250x affinity of O2 → carboxyhemoglobin (COHb) → tissue hypoxia
options.explanation: CO binds hemoglobin with 250x affinity of O2 → carboxyhemoglobin (COHb) → tissue hypoxia

options.text: Requires invasive testing in all cases

mcqs.question: What is the KEY diagnostic or management consideration for Carbon Monoxide Poisoning?

options.text: Treatment: 100% O2 via NRB mask (reduces CO half-life from 5h to 1h); Hyperbaric O2 for: syncope, seizures, COHb >25%, pregnancy, cardiac ischemia
options.explanation: Treatment: 100% O2 via NRB mask (reduces CO half-life from 5h to 1h); Hyperbaric O2 for: syncope, seizures, COHb >25%, pregnancy, cardiac ischemia

options.text: Immediate surgical intervention in all cases

mcqs.question: A patient presents with findings consistent with Carbon Monoxide Poisoning. Which finding would you expect?

options.text: Pulse oximetry reads FALSELY NORMAL (cannot distinguish COHb from OxHb)
options.explanation: Pulse oximetry reads FALSELY NORMAL (cannot distinguish COHb from OxHb)

mcqs.stem: A patient presents 2 hours after ingesting an unknown quantity of acetaminophen. Serum APAP level at 4 hours post-ingestion is 220 mcg/mL. Using the Rumack-Matthew nomogram, what is the appropriate management?

options.text: Start IV N-acetylcysteine (NAC) — the level is above the treatment line
options.explanation: On the Rumack-Matthew nomogram, the treatment line at 4 hours is 150 mcg/mL (FDA line) or 200 mcg/mL (original Rumack-Matthew line). At 220 mcg/mL, the level exceeds both thresholds. IV NAC (150 mg/kg loading, then 50 mg/kg over 4h, then 100 mg/kg over 16h) should be started immediately.

options.text: Recheck level at 8 hours before deciding on treatment
options.explanation: With a clearly supratherapeutic level at 4 hours, treatment should begin immediately. Delaying NAC beyond 8 hours post-ingestion significantly increases risk of hepatotoxicity.

options.text: Administer activated charcoal only — NAC is not indicated within 4 hours of ingestion
options.explanation: Activated charcoal may be beneficial if given within 1-2 hours of ingestion, but it is NOT a substitute for NAC when the level indicates toxicity. NAC should be started as soon as possible.

options.text: Obtain LFTs and treat only if transaminases are elevated
options.explanation: Hepatotoxicity from acetaminophen is delayed (ALT typically rises at 24-72 hours). Normal LFTs at 4 hours do not exclude impending liver injury. The nomogram guides treatment BEFORE liver damage occurs.

references.title: AACT Position Paper: Single-Dose Activated Charcoal
